segments.s764-g97: تو is an emphatic/explicit second-person singular pronoun addressing the addressee. اکنون is an adverb 'now' modifying the command. بدین is poetic/contracted for به این ('to this'); here it points to the present festivity or mood. خرمی is the noun 'merriment/delight' that is the goal of the motion. بازگرد is an imperative (or jussive) verb 'return' directed at تو. Together they form an imperative clause: 'You, now, return to this merriment.'
segments.s764-g98: که introduces an explanatory/casual clause here ('for' / 'because'). جای سخن نیست is a fixed predicative phrase: جای ('place') + سخن ('speech/words') + نیست ('is not') = 'there is no place/time for words'. روز نبرد is a genitive noun phrase 'the day of battle' (classical Persian often omits an explicit ezafe linking). The whole segment gives the reason for the imperative: 'for it is the day of battle, there is no time for talk.'
segments.s764-g99: بگویش is the imperative 'tell/say to him' (بگو + -یش, the enclitic pronoun 'to him'). که introduces the content clause 'that'. در جنگ is a locative prepositional phrase 'in battle'. مردن is the infinitive/noun 'to die' (used as the act of dying). به نام is an idiomatic phrase 'for a name' = 'for the sake of fame/reputation' (به + نام). The segment thus issues the order to report: 'Tell him that dying in battle for the sake of fame...'.
segments.s764-g100: به از is an archaic/comparative formula meaning 'better than' (به ... از = 'better than'). زنده دشمن is the noun phrase 'the enemy alive' (zende = alive). بدو is the clitic form of به + او = 'to/for him' or 'toward him' (here indicating the enemy's relation to the addressee). شادکام is an adjective 'happy/fortunate' describing the enemy. The whole comparative clause: '...is better than the enemy being alive and rejoicing over him.'
segments.s764-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before its noun. جنی سخن گفت is an example of poetic word order: the phrase 'سخن گفت' ('spoke words') uses گفت in the past, third-person singular, with the object (جنی) placed before the predicate. وز آدمی uses وز as a linking particle equivalent to 'and' (poetic/contracted), connecting 'jinn' to 'آدمی' ('mankind').
segments.s764-g18: ز introduces a source or origin here ('from'); گفتار is a noun meaning 'utterance/speech' and takes the following genitive modifier پیغمبر هاشمی — 'the Hashemite prophet' (پیغمبر + adjectival/nisba هاشمی). Grammatically this phrase supplies the source or authority of what was said and links back to the verb گفت in the previous segment.
segments.s764-g19: This is a coordinate list introduced by the poetic preposition ز 'from'. توحید, قرآن, وعد and وعید are coordinated by و 'and'. The nouns are abstract religious terms; the sequence lists sources or items being enumerated without an explicit finite verb in this line (the verbal sense ties back to the surrounding clauses).
segments.s764-g20: ز again introduces items in the list: تأیید (here meaning 'confirmation'/'support') is followed by a coordinating و 'and', then another ز + رسمهای جدید ('from new customs'). رسمهای is the plural construct form (رسم + -های) and جدید is a postpositive adjective 'new' modifying رسمها. The repeated ز is emphatic/poetic, marking each item as coming 'from'.
segments.s764-g21: Another triadic list introduced by ز 'from': قطران ('tar') and آتش ('fire') are coordinated; زمهریر is a compound use with ز + مهریر (here meaning 'bitter cold' or freezing cold). Word order places the preposition before each item for rhythm and emphasis typical of Ferdowsi's verse.
segments.s764-g22: ز فردوس begins a parallel list: فردوس 'paradise' is followed by وز and another item. Here وز functions as a poetic connector equivalent to 'and'; grammatically حور ('houris') and جوی شیر ('stream of milk') are coordinated, where جوی ('stream') takes شیر ('milk') as its modifier (milk-stream). The repeated prepositions keep each item framed as a source/possession.
segments.s764-g23: ز introduces another cluster: کافور 'camphor' appears with منشور which here is a noun following it (rendered as 'inscribed tablet' in the senses provided). The coordinating و links to ماء معین: ماء 'water' modified by معین (here 'appointed'/'specified' or possibly a proper name). This is a rhythmic enumerative construction; each noun phrase is short and linked by conjunctions.
segments.s764-g24: درخت بهشت is a noun + genitive modifier 'the tree of paradise' (درخت + بهشت). The following و links می‌و ('fruit') and انگبین ('nectar/ambrosia'); انگبین is a classical word for the sweet nectar. As with the other lines, the items are presented as a series of delights or things 'from' (the repeated ز earlier governs this poetic list).
segments.s764-g9: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' placed before the following noun. دیبا is a noun 'brocade' that functions as the reason introduced by ز. نگویند is an archaic/poetic negative third-person plural of گفتن ('they do not say / they do not call'); here it negates the idea that material trappings make a 'مردانِ مرد'. مردان مرد is an emphatic epithet (plural noun + appositive noun) meaning 'real men' or 'heroes' (literally 'men of men').
segments.s764-g10: A list of reasons introduced repeatedly by the poetic preposition ز ('from / because of'): each ز + noun names an external, material thing that does not make a man a true man. The conjunction و links the items. خواب and خورد are nouns here ('sleep' and 'food' respectively), not verbs; the repetition of ز before each item is a classical poetic device for emphasis.
segments.s764-g11: گرانمایه is an adjective/adverbial compound 'precious / valuable' qualifying the following noun. پیروز here functions as an epithet/noun 'the victorious (one)' or title; نامه is the direct object 'letter'. بداد in classical usage can be a past-tense verb 'gave' (ب- + داد), so the clause reads 'the precious/venerable victor gave the letter.'
segments.s764-g12: سخنهای (the plural possessive form 'his/her/its words' or 'the words') + رستم identifies what is remembered. همی‌کرد is the habitual/past continuous auxiliary-like form (used in epic Persian) meaning 'was doing' or 'would do' and here combines with یاد to mean 'recalled/remembered.' یاد is a noun 'remembrance'; together the phrase means 'he remembered/was calling to mind Rostam's words.'
segments.s764-g13: سخنهاش is a contracted possessive form ('his/its words'); بشنید is simple past 'he/she/they heard.' The conjunction و links the next verb phrase: نامه بخواند is past simple 'he read the letter' or 'he caused the letter to be read.' Word order places the object before the verb, a common pattern in older Persian.
segments.s764-g14: دران ('in that' / 'at that') introduces the circumstance — 'in that telling/recital.' گفتن here is a verbal noun/infinitive 'the telling/recitation' of the نامه (letter). خیره بماند is a compound verb phrase: خیره 'staring/astonished' + بماند ('remained' / 'was left'), so the whole clause means 'he remained astonished' or 'was left staring.' The order (circumstance + verbal noun + predicate) is typical of the epic style.
segments.s764-g15: بتازی is a verbal adverb/participle from تازیدن ('to rush/charge') meaning 'rushing' or 'with haste' and sets the action's manner. یکی is an indefinite 'one / someone / a certain person' performing the action. نامه پاسخ نوشت is a straightforward past clause 'wrote an answer to the letter' (نامه = object, پاسخ = 'answer' as direct object or apposition, نوشت = 'wrote').
segments.s764-g16: پدیدار کرد is a causative/perfect-like construction 'made appear' or 'revealed.' اندرو ('in it' / 'therein') is the locative adverb indicating where the action took place. خوب و زشت are contrasted nouns/adjectives 'the good and the bad' that are the content made manifest. The verb کرد governs the whole phrase: 'he revealed therein the good and the bad.'
segments.s764-g1: چو + past verb = 'when' introducing a temporal clause; بشنید is a 3rd‑person past ('he heard'). آن گرانمایه مرد is a noun phrase with the demonstrative آن 'that' followed by the adjective گرانمایه 'esteemed/noble' modifying مرد 'man/hero'. Word order is typical older Persian: temporal marker → verb → subject → NP.
segments.s764-g2: پذیره شدش is a compound predication: پذیره (reception/acceptance) with شدش ('it became his' / 'he received him') — the enclitic -ش marks the object 'him'. با سپاهی is a prepositional phrase 'with an army', and چو گرد is a simile 'like a ring/around' modifying how the troops were arranged.
segments.s764-g3: فرود آوریدندش is the past plural of the compound verb فرود آوردن 'to bring down/bring in', with the object enclitic -ش 'him'. اندر زمان is a temporal phrase 'in/at the time' (i.e. 'at that moment').
segments.s764-g4: بپرسید is a past tense verb 'he asked' placed before the subject (inverted order common in poetry); سعد is the subject. از governs the following noun phrase and here means 'about/concerning'; تن is used in the sense of 'person/self' (asking about his person/identity); پهلوان 'champion' names whose person is being queried.
segments.s764-g5: هم 'also' adds the item to a list. از before شاه and دستور means 'about/concerning' those things; the repeated و links items. ز (poetic variant of از) before لشکرش likewise means 'about his army' and the suffix -ش marks 'his'.
segments.s764-g6: ز again 'about/from' introduces سالار بیدار 'the watchful/alert commander' (noun + adjective), then و links and ز کشورش 'and about his country' where -ش is the possessive clitic 'his'.
segments.s764-g7: ردا 'cloak' is the direct object of بفگند (an archaic/spelling variant of بفکند) 'they cast/they threw'; زیر 'under/beneath' marks location and پیروز is the noun 'the victor' (or 'victorious one'). The clause ends with و گفت 'and said' (here, ו + past verb); note the lexical list for گفت lacks the expected 'said' sense, so the correct reading in context is the past-3rd 'he/they said'.
segments.s764-g8: که introduces reported speech 'that / (saying) that'. ما داریم is the speaker's clause 'we have'; نیزه و تیغ are the items listed (spear and sword) and جفت 'pair' completes the noun phrase 'a pair (of them)'. In narrative this is reported utterance, so present 'داریم' is natural as quoted speech.
segments.s764-g49: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. شعبه مغیره is a personal name (Shu'ba Mughīra) functioning as the subject that follows چو. بگفت is the simple past narrative verb 'said' (ب‑ prefix + گفت), and آن زمان is a noun phrase 'that time' specifying when he spoke.
segments.s764-g50: که introduces a subordinate reported-clause 'that/which'. آید is the present/subjunctive form of آمدن used here to report the coming ('would/comes'); بر is a preposition meaning 'against' or 'upon' (here 'against/to' in the sense of meeting for combat), and رستم پهلوان is the object phrase 'Rostam the champion'.
segments.s764-g51: ز ایران is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from Iran' (ز = از). یکی نامداری uses یکی as an indefinite 'a certain / one' and نامداری functions as a descriptive noun 'a famed/notable one'. ز راه is an adverbial 'by way of / by the road' describing his route.
segments.s764-g52: بیامد is the past tense 'came' (poetic form). بر here governs the following noun phrase and means 'toward/to' (approaching). پهلوان سپاه is the target phrase 'the hero's army' (سپاه as 'army/host'); word order is noun + modifier, so 'toward the hero, (i.e. his) army'.
segments.s764-g53: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/that came'. آمد is past 'came'. فرستاده‌ای is the noun 'a sent one / envoy' formed from فرستاده (here used as 'messenger'); پیر و سست are coordinating adjectives 'old and weak' describing that envoy.
segments.s764-g54: The negative pairing نه ... و نه ... lists what he lacked: نه اسپ و سلیح 'neither horse nor weapon'. The final phrase نه چشمی درست means 'nor a proper/trustworthy eye' (چشمی 'eye/eyewitness', درست 'proper/correct'), i.e. lacking reliable sight or a trustworthy look/companion.
segments.s764-g55: یکی here is the indefinite numeral 'a/one' introducing تیغ باریک 'a thin blade'. بر گردنش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his neck' (بر + گردن + possessive -ش = 'on his neck'), with word order noun + possessive suffix.
segments.s764-g56: پدید آمده is a compound perfect/resultative: پدید 'appear' + آمده 'has come' = 'has appeared/has come into view'. چاک پیراهنش is the noun phrase 'the tear/gash of his shirt' (چاک 'gash/tear', پیراهن 'shirt', -ش possessive 'his').
segments.s764-g41: Poetic word order: جهانی کجا = 'in the world, where' (جهانی used adverbially). شربتی آب سرد is a noun phrase 'a cold cup/glass of sherbet' with the adjective سرد following the noun آب (typical Persian order). The whole segment asks rhetorically 'where in the world is there a cold sherbet (worth...)?'.
segments.s764-g42: نیرزد is a present/pseudo-present predicate meaning 'is not worth' (verb before object by poetic inversion). دلت را is the direct object with the 2nd-person suffix -ت attached to دل and followed by object marker را. چه داری به درد is a question 'what do you have for/that causes pain?' (داری = you have; به درد = 'to/for pain' = 'cause to suffer').
segments.s764-g43: هرآنکس که introduces a relative/conditional 'whoever'. پیش من آید به جنگ = 'comes before me to fight/into battle', with آمدن (آید) in present/future sense; پیش = 'in the presence of' or 'before'. The clause sets up the someone who challenges the speaker.
segments.s764-g44: نبیند is an archaic/persianized present/future form with the negative-prefix ن- + بیند 'see', here functioning idiomatically as 'will see nothing'; followed by به جز 'except' to yield 'will see nothing but...'. دوزخ و گور تنگ = 'hell and a narrow grave' (تنگ qualifies گور).
segments.s764-g45: بهشتست = 'is paradise' (copular statement). اگر بگروی is a conditional 'if you adopt/accept (it)' where ب- prefix + گرویدن (بگروی) is an archaic subjunctive/imperative-form indicating 'you embrace/convert'. جای تو = 'your place' (subject/thing whose status is being predicated).
segments.s764-g46: نگر is an imperative ('look' / 'consider'). تا here is conjunction meaning 'so that / in order to' (common in classical Persian: 'look so that...'), introducing the subordinate clause چه باشد کنون رای تو — چه باشد = 'what may be', کنون = 'now', رای تو = 'your opinion/decision'. باشد is subjunctive/correlative 'may be' used in indirect question.
segments.s764-g47: به قرطاس مهر عرب برنهاد is a transitive verb phrase: به قرطاس = 'onto the paper', مهر عرب = 'the Arab's seal' (مهر as 'seal'), and برنهاد (برداشتن/نهادن with prefix بر-) is a past/classical perfective 'placed on / affixed'. Word order is object–verb typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s764-g48: درود محمد همی‌کرد uses همی‌کرد (classical habitual imperfect) meaning 'he would / kept on doing' — here 'kept saying/recalling the greeting'. درود محمد = 'greeting/blessing of Muhammad' (an invocation), and یاد here means 'remembrance' (he kept the greeting in his memory or repeatedly uttered it).
segments.s764-g57: چورستم is the fused archaic form of چو رستم = “when Rostam”; چو here functions as the subordinating conjunction “when/as.” به گفتار او uses به + noun to mark the target of attention (‘to/at his speech’), and بنگرید is a literary past form (بـ + نگر + -ید) meaning ‘looked/gazed’ or ‘regarded.’ Together they form a temporal clause: “When Rostam looked at his words…”
segments.s764-g58: ز is the poetic/prepositional form meaning ‘from’ or ‘out of.’ دیبا سراپرده is a compound noun (dība + sara-parde) meaning ‘the brocade/dība curtain’ (the fabric is the head noun modified by sara-parde), and برکشید is a past-tense verb ‘he pulled up/drew aside’ (بر + کشید). The subject is carried over from the previous clause, so the verb describes the action that follows Rostam’s looking.
segments.s764-g59: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from.’ زربفت چینی is a nominal phrase: زربفت ‘gold-brocade’ modified by چینی (‘Chinese’ here, an adjectival origin), so ‘from the Chinese gold-brocade.’ کشیدند is the 3rd-person plural past ‘they pulled/drew,’ and نخ is the direct object ‘a thread/cord.’ The word order is typical: prepositional phrase + object + verb (they pulled a cord from the brocade).
segments.s764-g60: سپاه اندر آمد is a verbal clause: سپاه ‘the army/host’ + اندر آمد ‘entered/came in’ (اندر = in/within; آمد = came). چو introduces a simile ‘like/as,’ and مور و ملخ are the comparison terms ‘ants and locusts.’ The whole clause reads as a simile: the host entered like ants and locusts.
segments.s764-g61: نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن ‘to place/set down’ (here a verb: ‘they placed’). زرین is an adjective ‘golden’ modifying یکی زیرگاه: یکی ‘one/a certain’ and زیرگاه ‘a low seat/place’ (a compound meaning a low station or foot-level seat). Word order is verb (they placed) + adjective + noun phrase (a golden low-seat).
segments.s764-g62: نشست is the past form ‘sat’ (3rd-person singular). از برش here is an idiomatic locative: از + بر + ـش (his side) = ‘at/by his side,’ and پهلوان سپاه is a genitive phrase ‘the champion of the host/army.’ Together: ‘the champion of the army sat at his side.’
segments.s764-g63: بر او is a locative phrase ‘over/at him’ (here ‘beside him’ or ‘before him’). از ایرانیان introduces provenance/partitive ‘of/from the Iranians.’ شست مرد is a terse numerical/collective expression in older usage (here functioning to present a group of men); grammatically the clause puts the locative/partitive before the noun phrase to indicate who stood there. The verb is implied (stood/sat) from the narrative context.
segments.s764-g64: This is a coordinated noun phrase: سواران ‘horsemen’ + و ‘and’ + مردان ‘men/warriors’ with روز نبرد as a postmodifier (‘of the day of battle,’ i.e., battlefield veterans). The coordination lists the types of Iranian fighters present.
segments.s764-g65: به here functions adverbially as 'with/by', introducing the material; زر (gold) is the material noun, and بافته is a past-participle 'woven' that modifies جامه‌های (the following noun phrase). Word order places the material + participle before the noun it qualifies: 'garments woven with gold.'
segments.s764-g66: بپا is the fused form of به پا (here 'on the feet' / 'put on'); اندرون indicates location 'inside/within'; کرده is the perfect/past participle (literally 'had made/put') forming a periphrastic past with the preceding phrase; زرینه is an adjective 'golden' modifying کفش 'shoe'. Together this says he had put golden shoes on (his feet) inside.
segments.s764-g67: همه quantifies the following plural noun طوق داران ('those who have/ wear a necklace/collar'); داران is the plural/agentive element 'holders/wearers'. با گوشوار is a prepositional adjunct 'with earrings' placed after the head noun, describing how those people appear.
segments.s764-g68: سرا پرده here reads as 'the chamber's curtain(s)' (possession/genitive relationship); آراسته is a past-participle/adjective 'adorned' describing پرده, and شاهوار is a postpositive adjective 'regal/lofty' further qualifying the curtain. The sequence is noun + participial adjective + qualifying adjective.
segments.s764-g69: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; شعبه is the subject noun (here treated as a name or noun reaching something); به بالای is a prepositional phrase 'to the top/upper part of'; پرده سرای is a genitive construction 'the curtain of the chamber'. Together they mark the temporal/location setting 'when X reached the upper curtain of the chamber.'
segments.s764-g70: بیامد is simple past 'came'; بران (= برِ آن / بر آن) functions adverbially 'on/onto that' referring back to the curtain/spot; جامه is the noun 'garment' and ننهاد is the negative past of نهادن ('did not place/put'); پای 'foot' completes the verb phrase: 'he did not place his foot on that garment.' Note the negative prefix ن on the past stem نهاد ('did not set').
segments.s764-g71: همی‌رفت is an imperfect/progressive past ('was going'), where the prefix همی signals continuous aspect; برخاک is the directional adverb 'toward/onto the ground'; برخوار appears as a place-name (or directional phrase) and خوار is an epithet 'lowly/despised' modifying it. The phrase describes motion toward a place or the ground with an epithet attached.
segments.s764-g72: ز is the preposition 'from/by/with' (poetic z-); شمشیر is the instrument 'sword'; کرده is the past participle 'having made'; یکی is the numeral/indefinite 'one/a certain'; دستوار is an adjective 'steady/orderly' modifying یکی دست ('one steady hand'). Grammatically this is a participial/instrumental clause: 'by the sword having made one hand steady.'
segments.s764-g25: اگر introduces a conditional clause. شاه is the subject. بپذیرد is the 3rd-person singular present-subjunctive/potential form (بـ- prefix + root) used in Persian to mark the protasis (‘if … accepts’). این دین راست is the direct object phrase; راست is a postposed adjective meaning “true/right.”
segments.s764-g26: دو عالم (‘the two worlds’) is the topical subject (this idiom = this world and the next). به introduces the resultant state/condition (“in/under”), followed by a coordinated nominal predicate شاهی و شادی و راست (kingship, joy, and righteousness). The copula is omitted (common in poetic Persian) and is supplied by the earlier conditional.
segments.s764-g27: همان (‘that/the same’) modifies تاج. تاج دارد is a complete clause: تاج (object/topic) + دارد (3rd-singular present ‘has’). The second noun phrase همان گوشوار repeats همان for emphasis; its verb is omitted and shared with دارد (a common coordination in Persian).
segments.s764-g28: همه‌ساله is an adverbial phrase ‘every year/yearly’. با is the preposition ‘with’ introducing a list of accompanying qualities: بوی و رنگ و نگار (scent, color, and ornament). نگار here is used in the sense of decorative image/ornamentation rather than ‘beloved’.
segments.s764-g29: شفیع از گناهش is a topical phrase: شفیع ‘intercessor’ specified with از گناهش ‘with respect to his sin/for his sin’. محمد بود is the comment/predicate: محمد + بود (past copula ‘was’), so: ‘Muhammad was the intercessor for his sin.’
segments.s764-g30: تنش is ‘his body’ (تن + possessive suffix -ش). چون is a simile marker ‘like/as’. گلاب مصعد is a genitive/attributive pairing where مصعد (‘place/point of ascent’) qualifies گلاب (‘rosewater’), i.e. ‘the rosewater of the ascent’; بود is the past copula ‘was’. The phrase says his body was like the rosewater of ascent (pure/fragrant and fit for ascent).
segments.s764-g31: بکاری is an exhortative/subjunctive form of کاشتن (‘to plant/sow’), here understood as a general imperative (‘sow’/‘do’). که introduces the purpose/result clause ‘so that…’. پاداش یابی is a subjunctive/light-optative construction: یابی (subjunctive of یافتن) ‘you may obtain/attain’ the noun پاداش (‘reward’). بهشت is the goal ‘Paradise’. The sequence expresses purpose/result (‘sow so that you may win the reward of Paradise’).
segments.s764-g32: نباید is an impersonal modal ‘one must not/should not’. به باغ بلا is a locative/directional phrase ‘into/to the garden of affliction’ (به + باغ + بلا). کینه کشت places the object noun کینه (‘hatred, grudge’) before the verb کشت (poetic/older-form of کاشتن ‘to sow’); Persian allows noun-before-verb order, and here کشت functions as the verbal infinitive/verb meaning ‘to sow’. The whole clause: ‘(one) must not sow hatred in the garden of affliction.’
segments.s764-g33: تن یزدگرد و جهان فراخ is a short noun phrase with two coordinated parts. تن یزدگرد is a possessive/juxtaposed phrase meaning “the body (or person) of Yazdegerd” (Persian often omits the ezāfe in poetry), then و joins it to جهان فراخ, where the adjective فراخ follows the noun جهان in normal Persian order to mean “the wide/vast world.”
segments.s764-g34: چنین introduces and qualifies the following list as “such” or “so (great)”: باغ و میدان و ایوان وکاخ is a simple enumerative list linked with و (and). Note وکاخ here is orthographic attachment of و+کاخ (and palace) common in older texts; the adjective (چنین) precedes the whole series.
segments.s764-g35: همه تخت گاه و همه جشن و سور is anaphoric repetition: همه modifies each coordinated noun phrase (“all the throne-places” and “all the feasts and banquets”). تخت گاه is a compound-like pairing (تخت + گاه) meaning the place/seat of the throne; the second همه again scopes over جشن و سور.
segments.s764-g36: نخرم is the 1st-person negative form of خَریدن in classical Persian used here as “I would not (buy/ purchase).” به introduces the object or purpose/price phrase (here “for/for the sake of”), دیدار is “the sight/meeting,” and یک موی حور literally “one hair/lock of a ḥūrī.” Together they mean “I will not buy (all that) for the sight of a single houri’s lock.”
segments.s764-g37: دو چشم تو is the noun phrase “your two eyes” with the possessor تو after the noun (poetic order). اندر is the archaic/prepositional form of در meaning “in/within.” سرای here should mean “house/chamber” (poetic سرای = house), but the provided lemma senses do not include that sense; سپنج is a descriptive noun/adjective (literally “sponge”) modifying the place — a poetic epithet for the chamber.
segments.s764-g38: چنین خیره شد is a small clause: چنین (so/thus) modifies خیره (fixed/staring) and شد is the copular/auxiliary “became” — “became thus fixed.” از پی is a prepositional combination meaning “after/in pursuit of” or “because of following,” governing the objects تاج و گنج (“crown and treasure”).
segments.s764-g39: بس is an intensifier here (’enough’/’very’), modifying ایمن شدستی, an archaic 2nd-person perfect/past form meaning “you became secure” (شدستی = you became). بر + ین (here written as separate pieces) yields برین meaning “on that/this” and modifies تخت عاج (“ivory throne”). The noun–adjective order تخت عاج is normal (throne of ivory).
segments.s764-g40: بدین ... و بدین ... is a repetitive instrumental expression meaning “by/with these” or “with such” (classical usage). The list yوز و باز lists royal hunting animals (yūz = cheetah, باز = hawk/falcon), and سپس مهر و تاج names the favour/affection and the crown as further supports; the initial ب (or بدین) functions as a prepositional/instrumental marker in the poetic diction.
segments.s764-g89: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). سعد is the subject (the name Saʿd). با + تاج + ساسان forms an instrumental/genitive phrase 'with the Sasanian crown' (تاجِ ساسانی). بدی here is an archaic past verb form (from دادن) meaning 'gave' (the object 'you' is implied); the line uses older poetic morphology instead of Modern Persian داد/دادَ or دادَند.
segments.s764-g90: مرا (written here as م + را) is the object 'me/for me' (را marks the definite object). رزم 'battle' is the noun governed by کردن ('to make/cause'): رزم کردن = 'to fight / to make battle'. او is the third-person pronoun 'him'. کردن is the light verb 'to do / to make' used with رزم. آسان is the predicative adjective 'easy'. بدی at the end is again the same archaic past-form verb (here meaning 'became/was made' or 'you made it'), so the whole clause reads 'you made fighting him easy for me.'
segments.s764-g91: ولیکن is a formal/concessive conjunction 'but/yet'. بدَان is composed of the prefix ب- + دان 'know' and here works as an imperative/verb meaning 'know/consider'. کاخترَت is 'your palace/throne' (کاخ + possessive -ت). بی‌وفاست is the adjective بی‌وفا 'faithless' with the copula fused as -است ('is faithless').
segments.s764-g92: چه گوییم is a rhetorical question 'what shall I/we say' (چه = 'what' in an exclamatory/interrogative sense; گوییم is 1st-person-plural/present-subjunctive or optative used rhetorically). کامروز روز بلاست = 'this day is a day of calamity': کامروز = 'this very day / today', روز 'day', بلاست 'is calamity' (بلا = calamity, with -است fused).
segments.s764-g93: تو is the addressee 'you' (vocative/subject of address). را‌گر is a conditional particle meaning 'if' (archaic/poetic variant). محمد بود is a counterfactual/past-tense clause 'were Muhammad' or 'had Muhammad been' (بود = 'was/been'). پیش رو literally 'before/in front' (here 'in your presence' or 'standing before you'). The whole unit means 'if Muhammad had been before you'.
segments.s764-g94: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase به + این meaning 'in this way / thus'. کهن is the adjective 'old/ancient' qualifying 'manner'. گویم is 1st-person-singular present-subjunctive/optative 'I would say / I would speak' (here used to express a hypothetical/action under the conditional). از introduces the topic 'about/concerning', دین 'religion', نو 'new'. Together: 'I would thus, in this ancient way, speak about the new religion.'
segments.s764-g95: همان is an emphatic demonstrative 'that/the same'. کژ is an adjective 'crooked/askew' modifying پرگار 'compass' (here used metaphorically). این گوژپشت points to 'this hunchback' (این = 'this', گوژپشت = 'hunchback'). The phrase lists or apposes the images ('that crooked compass, this hunchback').
segments.s764-g96: بخواهد is the modal/optative of خواستن 'to will/wish' used here as 'would/will (do)'. همی‌بود is an old-form imperfect/conditional 'would be'. با ما 'with us' or 'toward us'. درشت is an adjective 'coarse/rough/harsh'. The clause says that this hunchback 'would be harsh toward us'.
segments.s764-g81: ازو (az-ū) is a prepositional pronoun 'from him/it' placed before the noun; نامه 'letter' is the definite/known object; بستد is the past-tense verb 'sealed/closed' (classical past form of بستن) and بخواننده is a dative/formal goal 'to the reader/reciter' (بـ + خواننده). داد is the past of دادن 'gave' and completes the compound action: 'sealed ... and gave (to) ...'.
segments.s764-g82: سخنها 'the words/speeches' is the plural topic; برو کرد here is a classical/poetic combination where برو (literally 'forth/go') + کرد (past of کردن 'to make/do') yields 'set forth' or 'caused to go forth'; خواننده is the agent 'the reciter' (who performs/voices the words) and یاد is the resulting noun 'remembrance/memory'—so the clause says the reciter made the words into a remembrance.
segments.s764-g83: چنین 'thus' introduces the manner; داد پاسخ is a fixed classical phrasing 'gave an answer' (داد = gave, پاسخ = answer). The particle که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'saying that'), and او را بگوی (written here as او رابگوی) is an imperative/infinitival instruction 'tell him' (او را = him, بگوی = tell/say to).
segments.s764-g84: که here continues the content clause 'that...' and introduces what should be told. نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. شهریاری is the abstract noun 'sovereignty/royal rule' and دیهیم functions as 'villager/peasant' (a rural lot). جوی in this high-register context denotes 'seek/search' (i.e., do not seek that), so the whole clause means 'that he should seek neither kingship nor a villager's lot.'
segments.s764-g85: ندیده is a past-participial/adverbial form 'having not seen' (used adverbially = 'without seeing'); سرنیزه‌ات is a possessed noun 'your spear-point' (سرنیزه + ـات 2nd-person suffix). بخت را is fronted here with the object marker را attached to بخت 'fortune/lot'—the whole phrase is an adverbial/fronted phrase setting time/condition ('before having seen the tip of your spear').
segments.s764-g86: دلت is دل 'heart' plus the 2nd-person suffix -ت = 'your heart' functioning as the grammatical subject. آرزو کرد is the simple past 'wished/longed'; مر تخت را is the object phrase 'the throne for me' (مر = to me / for me in classical usage, تخت = throne) with را marking definiteness/emphasis. Together: 'your heart wished (for) my throne.'
segments.s764-g87: سخن 'speech/words' is the subject; نزد دانندگان is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the knowledgeable' (نزد = before/in the presence of, دانندگان = the knowing/learned ones). خوارنیست is a fused predicate meaning 'is not low/contemptible' (خوار = low/contemptible, نیست = is not), so 'words are not to be despised before the learned.'
segments.s764-g88: تو را is the topicalized direct object 'you' with the object marker را (here marking the person who is being excluded). اندرین 'in this / among these' + کار 'affair/matter' forms 'in this affair'; دیدار نیست literally 'there is no meeting/appearance' or 'there is no place to be seen'—together meaning 'you have no place/role in this matter (no audience/meeting for you).'
segments.s764-g73: نشست is a past-tense verb 'sat' (here not the noun 'session'); از بر خاک is a somewhat archaic prepositional phrase meaning 'upon the earth/ground' with از + بر framing the location.کس را uses کس ('no one') with the object marker را to mark it as the (definite/emphatic) object, and ندید is the past negative 'did not see'. Word order is Subject-Verb then prepositional phrase and object marked by را.
segments.s764-g74: سوی ... ننگرید is a directional phrase with the verb at the end (typical Persian word order). سوی introduces the direction 'toward', پهلوان سپه is a noun–noun grouping 'champion of the host/army' (noun modifying noun), and ننگرید is a past-tense/look verb ('looked'/'gazed') in an older/poetic form (the initial ن- here is not a negation but part of the classical verbal form).
segments.s764-g75: بدو گفت رستم is 'Rostam said to him' where بدو is a dative/benefactive clitic 'to him' and گفت is the past 'said' (the lemma senses listed are nominal; here the verb sense 'said' is the intended meaning). که introduces the content/wish clause. جان شاددار is an optative/jussive expression: جان 'soul' + شاددار 'be joyful' (a wishing/imperative-like form applied to 'جان').
segments.s764-g76: بدانش here functions as a continuation of the wish—'to/for him' or 'may it be for him' (بدان = 'to that/that' with the clitic -ش referring back to the person addressed). روان و تن are two nouns 'spirit/soul' and 'body', joined by و. آباد دار is a classical periphrastic jussive/imperative meaning 'keep prosperous' or 'may ... be well' (آباد adjectival 'prosperous' + دار forming a verbal/adversative formula); together the phrase is another blessing: 'may his/your spirit and body be well.'
segments.s764-g77: بدو گفت شعبه is 'Shobeh said to him' (بدو = 'to him'); here شعبه is the speaker's name or label in the poem (the lexical senses listed are general 'branch/section' but it functions as a proper name). که‌ای نیک‌نام is a vocative/exclamation: که‌ای = archaic/poetic 'O' + نیک‌نام 'well-named/well‑reputed' addressing the addressee as 'O famed one.'
segments.s764-g78: This is a conditional: اگر introduces the protasis ('if'). دین پذیری is a second‑person present/subjunctive/optative 'you accept the religion/faith' (پذیری = 'you accept'). The main clause uses شوم (first‑person optative/subjunctive 'I shall become') followed by شادکام 'fortunate/happy' — so the sentence means 'If you accept the faith, I will be/have been made happy.'
segments.s764-g79: بپیچید رستم is a past verb 'Rostam turned' or 'recoiled'; بپیچید is an older/poetic past form of 'to turn/withdraw'. ز گفتار اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from his speech/words' (ز = 'from', گفتار = 'speech', اوی = possessive 'his'). Word order places the verb then the source phrase.
segments.s764-g80: بروهاش here is a contracted/poetic locative referring to his countenance/face (literary morphology combining preposition + رو + pronoun); پرچین شد is 'became wrinkled/furrowed' (شد = 'became'), and و زرد روی coordinates an additional result 'and (he) became yellow‑faced/pale' (زرد روی = 'yellow‑faced' i.e. pale). The clause describes a physical change in Rostam's appearance.

sentences.s764-s49: You should now return to this merriment; there is no place for words — it is the day of battle.
sentences.s764-s50: Tell him that dying in battle for the sake of fame is better than the enemy being alive and rejoicing over him.
sentences.s764-s9: He spoke of jinn and of mankind, and (also) from the words of the Hashemite prophet.
sentences.s764-s10: From monotheism and the Qur'an and promises and warnings; from confirmation and from new customs.
sentences.s764-s11: From tar and from fire and from bitter cold; from paradise and from houris and from a stream of milk.
sentences.s764-s12: From camphor, an inscribed tablet, and the appointed water; (and) the tree of paradise and fruit and nectar.
sentences.s764-s5: They do not call a true man such because of brocade, or of rose and silver, or of sleep and food.
sentences.s764-s6: The precious victor gave the letter; he recalled Rostam's words.
sentences.s764-s7: They heard his words and read the letter; at that recital of the letter he stood astonished.
sentences.s764-s8: Rushing, someone wrote an answer to the letter; therein he made manifest the good and the bad.
sentences.s764-s1: When Saʿd heard of that noble man, he received him and surrounded him with troops.
sentences.s764-s2: They brought him down at that moment; Saʿd asked the champion about his person.
sentences.s764-s3: He also asked about the king and the administration and about his army, about the vigilant commander and about his country.
sentences.s764-s4: They cast a cloak beneath the victor and said, 'We have a pair of spear and sword.'
sentences.s764-s25: When Shu'ba Mughīra said at that time that the champion Rostam would come.
sentences.s764-s26: From Iran a certain renowned man came by the road toward the hero's army.
sentences.s764-s27: Who came as an old, feeble envoy — with neither horse nor weapon, nor a proper eye.
sentences.s764-s28: A thin blade at his neck has appeared; the tear of his shirt has become visible.
sentences.s764-s21: What cold cup of sherbet is there in the world worth your heart? Why do you have cause to suffer?
sentences.s764-s22: Whoever comes before me to fight will see nothing but hell and a narrow grave.
sentences.s764-s23: Your place will be paradise if you convert; look and see what your decision is now.
sentences.s764-s24: He affixed the Arab's seal to the paper; he would repeatedly utter the greeting 'peace to Muhammad' in memory.
sentences.s764-s29: When Rostam looked at his words, he drew up the brocade curtain.
sentences.s764-s30: They drew a cord from the gold-brocade cloth; the host entered like ants and locusts.
sentences.s764-s31: They set down a golden low-seat; the champion of the host sat at his side.
sentences.s764-s32: Beside him stood Iranians — horsemen and warriors of the day of battle.
sentences.s764-s33: He wore purple robes woven with gold, and inside had put on golden shoes.
sentences.s764-s34: All the collar-wearers, with earrings, had adorned the chamber's curtains in regal fashion.
sentences.s764-s35: When Shaʿba came to the upper curtain of the chamber, he did not set his foot upon that garment.
sentences.s764-s36: He was going down onto the ground at Barkhvar, the lowly; with the sword he had made one hand steady.
sentences.s764-s13: If the king accepts this true religion, the two worlds will be (placed) in kingship, joy, and righteousness.
sentences.s764-s14: He has that same crown, that same earring, each year with fragrance, color, and ornamentation.
sentences.s764-s15: Muhammad was the intercessor for his sin; his body, like rosewater, belonged to the place of ascent.
sentences.s764-s16: Sow (good deeds) so that you may obtain the reward of Paradise; one must not sow hatred in the garden of affliction.
sentences.s764-s17: Yazdegerd's body and the wide world — such gardens, squares, porches, and palaces.
sentences.s764-s18: I would not buy all the throne-places and all the feasts and banquets for the sight of a single lock of a houri's hair.
sentences.s764-s19: Your two eyes, in that sponge-like chamber, became so fixed, staring after crown and treasure.
sentences.s764-s20: You became quite secure on that ivory throne — with those cheetahs and falcons, and with that favour and crown.
sentences.s764-s45: If Saʿd gave you the Sasanian crown, making me fight him would be easy.
sentences.s764-s46: But know this: your palace is faithless; what shall I say — today is a day of calamity.
sentences.s764-s47: If Muhammad were before you, I would, in this old manner, speak of the new religion.
sentences.s764-s48: That same crooked compass — this hunchback would be harsh with us.
sentences.s764-s41: He sealed the letter from him and gave it to the reader; the reciter set the words forth as a remembrance.
sentences.s764-s42: He answered thus: 'Tell him that he should seek neither sovereignty nor a villager's lot.'
sentences.s764-s43: Without having seen the point of your spear, your heart longed for my throne.
sentences.s764-s44: Speech is not contemptible before the learned; you have no place in this affair's meeting.
sentences.s764-s37: He sat down on the ground and saw no one; he looked toward the army's champion.
sentences.s764-s38: Rostam said to him, 'May your soul be joyful; may your spirit and body be well.'
sentences.s764-s39: Shobeh said to him, 'O well‑renowned one, if you accept the faith, I shall be happy.'
sentences.s764-s40: Rostam turned away from his words; his face became furrowed and he grew pale.
